title: Managing the angora moult
subtitle: How angora rabbits moult, when to harvest fibre, and how to prevent wool block during the moult period.
excerpt: Angora rabbits moult two to four times a year, and the moult cycle determines when fibre can be harvested and when the rabbit is at greatest risk of wool block. Understanding the moult pattern, harvesting fibre at the right time, and managing the rabbit's diet and grooming routine during the loose-coat period prevents the most common cause of death in angoras.
sourceNotes: British Angora Rabbit Society: husbandry and fibre harvesting guidelines. Varga (2013), Textbook of Rabbit Medicine (2nd ed.), Butterworth-Heinemann: gastrointestinal stasis chapter. International Association of German Angora Rabbit Breeders: production standards.

glossaryTerms.definition: A form of gastrointestinal stasis in angora rabbits caused by ingestion of large quantities of loose moult fibre during self-grooming. Unlike cat hairballs, rabbits cannot vomit to expel fibre. The accumulated wool in the stomach can slow gut motility, leading to a potentially fatal blockage. Signs include reduced or absent droppings, reduced appetite, and a bloated abdomen. Prevented by harvesting fibre before it becomes loose and by supplementing with papaya enzyme or high-fibre roughage during the moult.

Harvesting angora fibre by hand by gently pulling the fully mature, loose fibre free from the coat at the peak of the moult. Plucking produces the longest, highest-quality fibre; the natural break in the shaft at the moult line means the fibres release easily when the coat is ready. Premature plucking pulls fibre that is not yet ready to release, is uncomfortable for the rabbit, and damages the root.

content.text: The moult cycle in angoras drives both the fibre production schedule and the main welfare risk.
kills more angoras than any other cause, and it is almost entirely preventable. The key is to harvest the coat before it becomes loose enough to be ingested in large amounts during grooming.

A moult begins at the head and neck and moves toward the rump over several weeks. The advancing moult line is visible as a parting in the coat: behind it the new shorter growth, in front of it the old full-length coat. When you lift the coat at the moult line, the old fibre releases easily from the follicle. This is the moment to harvest, not before.

is the most efficient and highest-quality harvest method. Hold a small section of coat between finger and thumb close to the skin, and draw the fibre gently outward in line with its direction of growth. Ready fibre releases with almost no resistance. If you feel significant pull, the coat is not ready at that point; move to an area where the moult line is more advanced.

Scissors or clippers work if plucking is not practical. Cut fibre has a blunt end rather than a tapered tip and is lower quality for handspinning. For a fibre-production flock, timed plucking at the natural moult gives the best results and is gentler on the coat.

During the loose-coat period: increase the proportion of hay in the diet to help move ingested wool through the gut. Reduce pellet allowance slightly to push hay intake up. A pinch of dried papaya powder or a piece of fresh pineapple core may help break down fibre in the gut, though the evidence is mostly anecdotal. The most important step is to reduce loose fibre by harvesting early and keeping the coat trimmed short around the face where self-grooming is heaviest.

attrs.title: Signs of gut stasis during moult

content.text: A reduced or absent dropping count, a rabbit sitting hunched in the corner of its pen, or a visibly bloated abdomen during the moult period are warning signs. Gut stasis requires prompt veterinary treatment: gut motility drugs, fluids, and often assisted feeding. Do not wait 24 hours before calling a vet. Angora gut stasis moves quickly.
